storyDialogue.text: The Kingdom of Ashenmere had not seen rain in seven hundred days. The rivers were dust. The forests were bone.
And in the great hall of the crumbling palace, the last heir to a dying throne knelt before a mirror that showed her nothing.
The mirror used to show the future. My grandmother could see empires rise and fall in its surface. My mother saw the plague that would take her.
I see nothing. Only glass. Whatever magic ran through this bloodline, it chose to end with me.
The doors groaned open. A figure in tattered armor stepped through, trailing dried blood and the smell of smoke.
Your Majesty. The eastern garrison has fallen. Lord Vael's forces hold the Ashen Bridge.
How many did we lose?
Two hundred and thirty-one souls. I know because I buried them myself, one by one, in soil too dry to accept the dead.
And the survivors?
Seventeen. They're in the courtyard now, drinking the last of the well water. By tomorrow, there won't be enough for anyone.
Then we have until tomorrow to find a way to save what's left of this kingdom.
The vault beneath the palace held secrets older than the kingdom itself. Maren descended alone, carrying a torch that guttered in the stale air.
My ancestors sealed this place after the Sundering. They said the things down here were too dangerous to use, and too important to destroy.
I always thought it was superstition. Stories to frighten children into obedience.
The torch illuminated a chamber lined with crystallized tears — each one containing a frozen scream from the age before memory.
Gods above and below.
You shouldn't have come down here alone. There are wards on these walls that haven't been maintained in centuries.
I ordered you to stay above.
And I disobeyed. Court-martial me when we're not facing extinction. Look — there, on the pedestal.
A crown. Not gold, not silver — but carved from a single piece of midnight stone, veined with something that pulsed like a dying heartbeat.
The Hollow Crown. I thought it was a myth.
Every myth in this vault seems uncomfortably real.
The old texts say it grants dominion over drought and storm. But the price... the wearer surrenders their mortality. Not death. Something worse. An eternity of conscious silence.
Then we leave it. We'll find another way.
There is no other way, Theron. You know that as well as I do.
They stood in the throne room as the last light of the dying sun bled through cracked stained glass. The crown sat between them like a wound.
I've served three rulers of Ashenmere. Your grandfather was cruel. Your mother was kind. You are neither — you are necessary.
And I am telling you, as the last soldier who still draws breath for this kingdom: do not put on that crown.
If I don't, seventeen people die of thirst by morning. The kingdom dies with them. Everything my family built across three hundred years becomes dust.
Better dust than what that thing will make of you.
You buried two hundred and thirty-one people today, Theron. Look me in the eye and tell me their deaths were acceptable.
I'm not asking your permission. I'm not asking for your blessing. I'm asking you to remember me as I am now. Before the crown takes what's left.
Maren—
Promise me.
I promise.
She lifted the Hollow Crown with both hands. It was lighter than air and heavier than grief. The stone pulsed once — twice — and went still.
Maren placed it upon her head. The drought broke. The sky wept. And the queen of Ashenmere never spoke again.
